Case Study • 2025

From Feedback to
427.4% ROI

How user research turned a struggling POS interface into measurable business value

427.4%
ROI
>50%
Time Saved
1000s
Daily Txns Impacted

→

Act 1 — The Problem
Product roadmap workshop in Malè, Maldives. A colleague raised a red flag:
👨‍💻
Colleague
"Cashiers are struggling with the new return process. They can't understand the design… but we don't know why."
The previous design had good intentions — but feedback from the field told a different story. Each extra second per return multiplied across thousands of daily transactions.

We found 4 pain points in the design

1
2
3
4
1Return Process — hidden, cashiers can't find it
2Day End — icon only, no text label
3Discounts — buried in menu → product click
4Reprint — hidden despite high frequency
These weren't cosmetic issues. Every hidden button, every missing label was costing the business time and money at scale.

So I reframed the design problem as a business question:

How do we reduce return processing time
while keeping the design scalable for future features?

Act 2 — The Research

Two-Step Research Approach

With limited design budget, I needed to invest research effort where the ROI would be highest. So I ran two parallel tracks:

Step 1 — Business Lens

Heat Map Analysis
Internal team voted on activity importance (1–5 scale) to understand business priorities.

Step 2 — User Lens

Direct Cashier Input
Same questions to real cashiers to understand operational reality.

🧠 Critical Insight
Where Business Needs Met User Needs
Overlaying both data sets revealed a strategic map:
High overlap (Total, Reprint, Void, Hold) → invest heavily, both sides agree.
User-only priority (Return, Receipt) → business undervalued these, but they drove the most cashier frustration.
Act 3 — The Design
The first iteration failed — but that failure saved us from a costly post-launch redesign.
Mid-fi prototypes tested with cashiers using real transaction scenarios — because design decisions needed to be validated against actual operational workflows, not assumptions.
Mid-Fi Prototype — menu restructured, activities exposed

Usability Test: Button Meaning Scores

Return
0.85
Reprint
0.78
Sale Flow
0.75
Discount
0.60
Hold/Recall
0.56
Day End
0.50

Final Design — What Changed

1
2
3
4
1Grouped Activities — Reprint/Recall/Void/Return together
2Sorted by Frequency — most-used in prime position
3Icon + Text — dual representation on every button
4Product-Level Actions — discount/edit at item level
Act 4 — The Results

Performance Before & After

Each second saved compounds across thousands of daily transactions per store

ActionBeforeAfterWhy It Matters
Return~1 min<30sMost vital; impacts customer wait
Day End~1 min<30sImpacts shift closing
Transactions~10s<10sMain action, consistency matters
Receipt Reprint~1 min<30sNeeded the most improvement

The business result:

427.4%
Return on Investment
50%+ time reduction on returns (60s → <30s)
Reduced training overhead — icon+text meant faster onboarding
Improved customer experience — shorter queues at checkout
Systematic adoption of new UI patterns across all POS workflows

Business × Design Lessons

Stakeholder reports ≠ Root cause
Colleagues reported symptoms, not causes. Investing in direct user research avoided redesigning the wrong thing.
Low frequency ≠ Low business impact
Returns were "rare" but each slow return = a customer waiting. Impact per incident was high.
Design debt compounds like financial debt
Assumptions like "returns are rare" created hidden costs that grew with every new store deployment.
Data wins stakeholder buy-in
Heat map scores made prioritization objective. Stakeholders agreed because it was evidence, not opinion.
💡 Key Takeaway
Design ROI is a business metric, not a design metric.
427.4% ROI didn't come from aesthetics. It came from understanding that every cashier interaction has a cost, quantifying that cost through research, and systematically eliminating friction where the business impact was highest.

Highlighted Skillsets

User Research Design Enhancement Stakeholder Collaboration
Data-Driven Prioritization Business Impact Analysis ROI Measurement

HARRY HEIN • SENIOR PRODUCT DESIGNER

#Business Insight
Compounding Cost
Every second saved compounds across thousands of daily transactions. Right fix needs root cause, not assumptions.
Operational Efficiency
#Business Insight
Cost of Friction
Slow return = longer queue = lost revenue per store, per day. Every extra second multiplies across thousands of daily transactions.
Operational Efficiency Principle
#UX Insight
Problem Reframing
Frame as “users confused” → deprioritized. Frame as “losing $X/day” → immediate executive attention.
Stakeholder Communication
#Business Insight
Resource Allocation
Limited design budget means you can't fix everything. Overlapping business + user priorities = highest ROI per design hour invested.
Strategic Prioritization
#UX Insight
Fail Fast, Save More
Prototype fix = hours. Post-launch fix = weeks + retraining hundreds of cashiers. Iteration is risk management.
Iterative Design ROI
#Business Insight
Usability = Training Cost
0.75+ = ship it. Below 0.60 = training cost risk. Training cost × stores = measurable expense that better design eliminates.
Cost-Benefit of Usability
#Business Insight
Compound Returns
427% ROI isn’t a one-time win. Every new store deployment, every new cashier hired automatically inherits these efficiency gains — the ROI compounds over time.
Scalable Design Impact
#UX Insight
Design as Investment
When you can quantify the cost of bad UX and the savings of good UX, design stops being a “nice-to-have” — it becomes a strategic business investment.
Design Leadership
